text: Hello friends, this is a case of inguinal hernia and we will perform transabdominal preperitoneal repair.
The surgeon prepares to perform transabdominal preperitoneal repair on a left-sided inguinal hernia, positioning the ipsilateral port for the procedure.
The video shows a complete inguinal escrotal indirect hernia, which is the target for the surgical repair.
The surgeon positions the telescope 24 centimeters away from the target area of the left-sided inguinal hernia, preparing for transabdominal preperitoneal repair.
The surgeon positions the telescope and surgical instruments at an 18-centimeter distance ipsilaterally, marking the first arc for the procedure.

The surgeon positions the supraumbilical camera port 24 centimeters from the target site, with two additional ipsilateral ports placed 7.5 centimeters laterally on each side. The ports are arranged according to the baseball diamond concept for the left-sided inguinal hernia repair, designating the right-hand and left-hand ports.

The surgeon has introduced all the ipsilateral ports and is now viewing the inside of the surgical field, revealing a large indirect inguinal scrotal hernia. The first step involves reducing the hernia's contents.

The surgeon uses two atraumatic graspers to gently pull down the omentum from the inguinal scrotal hernia. An assistant helps by manipulating the scrotum to facilitate the reduction of the hernia contents.
The surgeon gently manipulates the omentum to avoid bleeding, while the assistant applies pressure from above to safely extract the hernia contents.
The surgeon safely extracts the hernia contents from the inguinal scrotal hernia using atraumatic graspers, with assistance to stabilize the scrotum.
The surgeon removes the hernia contents from the sac and pulls them back into the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon has successfully reduced the hernia, with the contents now visible outside the sac and positioned within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon begins the procedure from the medial umbilical ligament using an ipsilateral port, preparing to address the reducible hernia.
The surgeon begins the procedure at the medial umbilical ligament, preparing to address the hernia repair.

The surgeon approaches the arc of the hernia, reaching six centimeters to the outer margin of the deep ring at the ten o'clock position. A harmonic scalpel is used for dissection, although cold scissors or scissors with energy could also be utilized.

The surgeon slowly moves the hernia content into the abdomen, then proceeds to create a preperitoneal pocket. A mesh is prepared, sized at twelve plus the defect, ensuring a total of six centimeters around the margin of the defect for proper placement.

The surgeon positions the teflon jaw of the harmonic scalpel towards the abdominal wall to avoid damaging the inferior epigastric vessel, while carefully separating the peritoneum from the transversalis fascia.

The surgeon makes a pre-peritoneal incision, followed by reducing the lateral and medial margins of the hernia defect. The area of the inferior epigastric vessel is visible, with the Teflon jaw of the harmonic scalpel positioned towards the abdominal wall to prevent vessel injury.

The surgeon begins the preperitoneal pocket incision at the medial umbilical ligament, carefully avoiding the creation of any buttonholes. The incision extends six centimeters laterally to the outer margin of the defect at the ten o'clock position.
The surgeon begins separating the peritoneum from the transversal fascia, working laterally to the inferior epigastric vessel in the area known as the triangle of pain.
Shack dissection we will do later.
Generally in our surgery we do lateral dissection first then medial dissection and then lastly shack dissection.
The surgeon performs lateral dissection, separating the peritoneum from the transversal fascia lateral to the inferior epigastric vessel, within the area known as the triangle of pain.
The surgeon performs blunt dissection of areolar tissue, aided by insufflated gas, within the preperitoneal pocket.
The surgeon completes the lateral dissection, separating the peritoneum from the transversal fascia lateral to the inferior epigastric vessel, approaching the area known as the triangle of pain.
The surgeon prepares to perform medial dissection following the completion of lateral dissection, transitioning to a new area of tissue manipulation.
The surgeon performs blunt dissection medial to the hernia sac, pushing the bladder inferiorly and medially to open the space of Retzius and visualize the Cooper's ligament.

The Cooper's ligament is visible, serving as a landmark. The surgeon continues to push the bladder medially and inferiorly while dissecting the hernia sac. The sac is carefully separated from the vas deferens and spermatic vessels in the case of a complete inguinal scrotal hernia.
The surgeon continues the dissection by moving slightly medial, using blunt dissection techniques to separate tissue layers and further expose anatomical structures.
The surgeon applies inferomedial traction with the left hand, revealing the Cooper's ligament beneath the inferior epigastric vessel.
The surgeon performs blunt dissection to achieve reduction of the inguinal scrotal hernia sac, carefully separating it from surrounding structures in a well-defined plane.
The surgeon continues dissection in the space of Radius, pushing the bladder inferiorly and medially to maintain exposure of the Cooper ligament.
The surgeon visualizes the Cooper's ligament, located above the space of Retzius, while pushing the bladder inferomedially.
The surgeon enters the space and performs sac dissection using two atraumatic graspers, carefully pushing the transverse fascia away from the hernia sac.
The surgeon separates the hernia sac from the pseudo sac, carefully distinguishing between the two structures within the surgical field.

In cases of large inguinal scrotal hernias, the surgeon may cut the hernia sac due to dense adhesions, leaving part of the sac in the scrotum. The transverse fascia is visible above, with the hernia sac located below.
The surgeon pulls the hernia sac inferomedially while pushing the transverse fascia upwards, with the assistant holding the structures in place.
The surgeon carefully manipulates the testes to prevent them from being pulled up towards the deep inguinal ring during the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon carefully manipulates the annular space, gradually pushing the transversal fascia upwards while the hernia sac descends.
The surgeon carefully separates the vas deferens and spermatic vessels from the hernia sac, ensuring these structures are distinct and free from the sac.
The surgeon displays the hernia sac, with the vas deferens and spermatic vessels visible below the sac.
The surgeon positions the vas deferens medially and the spermatic vessel laterally, demonstrating their spatial orientation relative to each other.
The surgeon continues the dissection of the hernia sac, carefully separating it from surrounding structures such as the vas deferens and spermatic vessels.
The surgeon creates a window between the hernia sac and the cord structures, visually separating these anatomical elements during the dissection.
The surgeon carefully separates the vas deferens and spermatic vessels from the hernia sac, using gentle manipulation to distinguish these structures.
The vas deferens and spermatic vessels are visible, positioned medially and laterally, respectively, as the dissection progresses.
The surgeon attempts to separate the hernia sac but finds it inseparable, with the vas positioned medially and the spermatic vessel laterally.
Sometime it doesn't separate.
So in that situation you may tranject it.
The surgeon prepares to cut the inseparable hernia sac, with the vase and spermatic vessel expected to become visible shortly.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to cut the tissue, preparing to expose the spermatic vessel and vase.
The surgeon visually identifies a complete inguinal throttle hernia, noting its presence in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a cutting instrument to transect the hernia sac, separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon enlarges the pocket by carefully separating the surrounding tissue, creating sufficient space for the placement of the base.
The surgeon enlarges the preperitoneal pocket, creating additional space for the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon identifies the spermatic vessel, which is positioned laterally within the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the vas deferens medially within the surgical field, adjacent to the previously noted spermatic vessel.
The surgeon is positioned over the triangle of doom, where the spermatic vessels and vas deferens are clearly visible.
The surgeon performs lateral dissection to further enlarge the preperitoneal pocket, creating additional space for the placement of the mesh base.
The surgeon introduces the telescope into the hernia sac, revealing the open transverse fascia within the sac.
The surgeon has completed the separation of the hernia sac, with the sac now fully isolated from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon observes the peritoneal side, noting the absence of any visible defects or openings, commonly referred to as a 'black hole.'
The sigmoid colon is visible protruding alongside the peritoneum.
The surgeon identifies the laterally positioned spermatic vessel, which is slightly separated from the surrounding peritoneum.

The camera view shows the interior of the hernia defect, with the cut part of the hernia sac clearly visible. There is no serum collection or bleeding present. The indirect inguinal hernia sac is fully exposed, revealing the triangle of zone, the spermatic vessel, and the vas deferens.
The surgical view shows the pubic symphysis, also known as the pubic community, clearly visible in the field.
The surgical view shows the triangle of pain, an anatomical region bordered by the iliopubic tract and the spermatic vessels, visible within the hernia repair site.
The transversus fascia and arc of the transversus abdominal muscle are visible above the surgical field.
The surgeon points out the inferior epigastric vessel, which is located above the previously identified anatomical structures.
The inferior epigastric vessel is visible within the surgical field, providing a clear anatomical landmark during the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon identifies the vas deferens within the surgical field.
The surgeon points out the inferior epigastric vessel, highlighting its location within the surgical field.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the hernia sac, revealing the spermatic vessel and clearly visualizing the inferior epigastric vessel. The mesh is now visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon spreads the mesh over the anatomical regions known as the triangle of doom, triangle of pain, and trapezoid of disaster, ensuring coverage over these critical areas during the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon ensures that the mesh covers all relevant anatomical structures, including the inferior epigastric vessel and spermatic vessel, within the surgical field.
The surgeon positions the mesh, ensuring that the peritoneum remains posterior to it, with the mesh covering the relevant anatomical areas.
The surgeon positions the mesh to extend six centimeters beyond each margin of the hernia defect, ensuring complete coverage over the area.
The surgeon positions the center of the mesh directly over the center of the hernia defect, ensuring proper alignment for coverage.
The surgeon prepares to use a tacker to secure the mesh in place, ensuring it covers the defect and extends beyond its margins.
The surgeon visually identifies the Cooper ligament medially and secures one tacker to it, ensuring the mesh is anchored in place.
The surgeon pushes the inferomedial margin of the hernia sac into the space of Retzius, ensuring proper positioning within the surgical field.
The surgeon places a tacker laterally on the transversus abdominis muscle and another on the rectus abdominis, just lateral to the inferior epigastric vessel, completing the mesh fixation.

The surgeon covers the mesh with the peritoneum, using a tacker to secure it in place. The hernia sac is petalized to provide additional peritoneal coverage over the mesh. The gas pressure is reduced to eight millimeters of mercury to facilitate the double bracing technique.

The surgeon covers the mesh with the peritoneum, ensuring no portion of the mesh is exposed to the abdominal wall. Care is taken to avoid firing the tacker over the inferior epigastric vessel. The procedure concludes with the removal of gas from the abdominal cavity.
